size: large (10-25 lb; one of the largest domestic cat breeds)

exercise.details: Maine Coons are playful and active but not hyperactive. Two to three 15-minute interactive play sessions daily keep them physically and mentally satisfied. They retain their kitten-like playfulness well into adulthood. They enjoy climbing, fetch, and interactive puzzle toys. Cat trees with high perches are important — they love to survey their territory from above. They adapt well to leash walking.

grooming.details: Maine Coons have a long, silky, semi-waterproof coat that is surprisingly tangle-resistant compared to other longhaired breeds, but still requires brushing 2-3 times weekly to prevent matting, especially in the chest ruff, armpits, and belly. Use a wide-tooth comb followed by a slicker brush. Their ear tufts and toe tufts need periodic trimming. They shed heavily in spring.

diet.notes: Maine Coons are large cats with correspondingly higher caloric needs than average. Feed a high-quality, protein-rich diet; wet food supports hydration and kidney health. They grow slowly and are not fully mature until age 3-5. Provide appropriate portion sizes as they can be prone to obesity if overfed. Fresh water always available.

health.vet_checkups: annually; cardiac ultrasound (echocardiogram) recommended from age 2+
health.notes: HCM (thickening of the heart muscle) is the most serious health concern in Maine Coons. A genetic mutation (MyBPC3) is testable via DNA. Annual echocardiograms from age 2 are recommended for early detection. HCM-tested breeding stock is the gold standard — ask breeders for test results.

temperament.notes: Maine Coons are friendly, sociable, and curious. They are one of the most dog-like cat breeds — they often follow their owners around, play fetch, and enjoy supervised outdoor adventures on a harness. They are not lap cats by nature but prefer to be near their people. They get along exceptionally well with children and dogs. Their chirping and trilling vocalizations are distinctive.

care_summary: Maine Coons are large, sociable cats with moderate grooming needs and a significant cardiac health risk. Annual echocardiograms from age 2 are the most important proactive health measure. Their gentle, dog-like temperament and adaptability make them one of the best family cat breeds.
